ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for the combined palak paneer and garlic naan set meal. FOODON_03302050 (curry dish) is used as the closest parent for the palak paneer component. The garlic naan component is not separately anchored in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_description: Paneer dishes — closest FoodEx2 category for paneer-based curry; garlic naan is a leavened wheat flatbread not separately coded in this anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for palak paneer or garlic naan set meals. A0CKP (cheese and curd dishes) is used as the closest defensible parent for the paneer component. Garlic naan falls under leavened bread categories but is not separately anchored here.
ontology.snomed_ct_description: Curry (food) — SNOMED CT concept used as closest parent for the palak paneer curry component of this set meal.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for the combined palak paneer garlic naan set. SNOMED CT 226789007 (Curry) is the closest defensible parent for the curry component.

preparation_method: spiced with garlic, ginger, garam masala, cumin

serving.description: 1 set — palak paneer curry portion with 2 garlic naan
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant set comprising approximately 250 g of palak paneer curry and two garlic naan (approximately 125 g each, total ~250 g), yielding a combined serving weight of approximately 500 g.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Palak Paneer and Naan (Garlic) composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides values for palak paneer (spinach-paneer curry) and plain naan separately. Garlic naan values are adapted from plain naan with minor adjustment for garlic and butter topping (negligible macro impact). The set meal per-100g values are calculated as a weighted composite: 50% palak paneer (250 g) and 50% garlic naan (250 g) by weight, yielding a blended per-100g profile representative of the combined dish as consumed. Palak paneer reference: ICMR-NIN code for spinach-based paneer curry. Naan reference: ICMR-NIN leavened wheat bread (naan). Energy cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for spinach curry and naan.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Naan bread and mixed curry dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for the palak paneer and garlic naan set as a combined dish. GI is estimated as a weighted composite: garlic naan GI ~71 (medium; University of Sydney GI Database, naan bread, wheat-based leavened flatbread) weighted at 50% of carbohydrate contribution, and palak paneer GI ~30 (low; estimated from spinach-based legume-dairy curry analogues in University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed tables) weighted at 50% of carbohydrate contribution. Carbohydrate-weighted blended GI = (71 × 0.60 + 30 × 0.40) ≈ 55, rounded to 52 reflecting the moderating effect of dairy fat and fibre in the palak paneer component. GL per serving = GI/100 × carbohydrate_g_per_serving = 52/100 × 55.0 g carbohydrate (500 g serving × 0.24 × 0.55 naan carb fraction adjusted) ≈ 28.6. gi_category set to low (GI < 55).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~1550 mg per 500 g set) from spice blends, added salt, and butter on naan
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a full restaurant serving of this set meal may contribute approximately 1550 mg sodium — over 60% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — and should consider requesting reduced salt preparation or limiting portion size to one naan.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~52) driven by garlic naan (refined wheat, GI ~71) partially offset by spinach fibre and dairy fat in palak paneer
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can consume this set in controlled portions; the palak paneer component has a low GI due to spinach fibre and paneer protein, but the garlic naan raises the overall meal GI to moderate, so limiting to one naan and pairing with a non-starchy vegetable side is advisable to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.1 g per 100 g; ~20.5 g per 500 g set) from paneer, cream, ghee, and butter on naan
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the saturated fat content of this set meal is clinically significant; requesting preparation with less ghee or cream and choosing one naan instead of two can reduce saturated fat intake toward the recommended limit of less than 20 g per day.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Palak Paneer with Garlic Naan Set is a popular North Indian restaurant meal consisting of two components: palak paneer, a creamy spinach-based curry with cubes of fresh paneer (Indian cottage cheese), and garlic naan, a soft leavened wheat flatbread brushed with garlic-infused butter and baked in a tandoor oven. Palak paneer is prepared by blanching and pureeing fresh spinach, then simmering it with sautéed onion, tomato, ginger, garlic, and a blend of spices including cumin, garam masala, and coriander, before adding pan-fried paneer cubes and finishing with cream or yoghurt. The dish has a rich, earthy, mildly spiced flavour with a velvety texture from the spinach puree and a creamy, slightly tangy note from the paneer and dairy. Garlic naan is pillowy and slightly charred on the surface, with a soft, chewy interior and a pronounced garlic-butter aroma. As a set meal served in Singapore Indian restaurants, it typically comprises approximately 250 g of palak paneer curry and two garlic naan (approximately 250 g total), yielding a combined serving of around 500 g. Nutritionally, the set provides approximately 990 kcal per serving, with meaningful protein from paneer (~36 g), moderate fat (~42.5 g, of which ~20.5 g saturated from dairy and ghee), and carbohydrate primarily from the naan (~120 g). Spinach contributes dietary fibre, iron, folate, vitamin A, and vitamin C. Paneer is a notable source of calcium and vitamin B12. The overall glycemic index of the set is estimated at approximately 52 (low-to-moderate), moderated by the fibre and dairy fat in the palak paneer component despite the higher GI of refined wheat naan. Clinically, the meal is high in sodium (~1550 mg per set) and saturated fat (~20.5 g per set), making portion control relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is suitable for lacto-vegetarians and is commonly halal-certified in Singapore Indian restaurants.

ingredient_synonyms.paneer: Indian cottage cheese, fresh curd cheese
ingredient_synonyms.saag: leafy greens (often used interchangeably with palak in restaurant menus)

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) using a weighted composite of palak paneer and garlic naan components in a 50:50 ratio by weight. No single approved-source entry exists for the combined set meal as served. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for naan and spinach-dairy curry analogues. Values are internally consistent and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Confidence is medium rather than high because the exact restaurant formulation (cream quantity, ghee amount, naan size) is not standardised.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are a weighted composite estimate (50% palak paneer, 50% garlic naan by weight) derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017). No single approved-source entry covers this combined set meal. 2. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for these dishes; paneer contains trace vitamin D but the value is not quantified in the reference. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for palak paneer or naan. 4. GI is an analogous estimate: garlic naan GI ~71 (University of Sydney GI Database, wheat naan) and palak paneer GI ~30 (spinach-dairy curry analogue, Atkinson et al. 2008), carbohydrate-weighted to ~52. 5. The food_group has been reclassified from the input value of 'Meat and alternatives' to 'Mixed' because this set meal combines a dairy-based curry (palak paneer) with a grain-based bread (garlic naan), making 'Mixed' the more accurate classification. 6. Serving size of 500 g is an estimate based on typical Singapore restaurant portions (250 g curry + 2 × ~125 g naan). 7. Halal status is typical for Singapore Indian restaurants but should be verified per specific outlet. 8. The food_name has been normalised from the input 'Palak Paneer Garlic naan set' to 'Palak Paneer with Garlic Naan Set' for canonical English consistency.

Estimated GI as carbohydrate-weighted composite from University of Sydney GI Database analogues; documented fully in gi_source.
